title: Poly(hydroxybutyrate-co-hydroxyvalerate) as a biodegradable binder in a negative electrode material for lithium-ion batteries
abstract_summary: This study evaluates poly(hydroxybutyrate-co-hydroxyvalerate) (PHBV), a biodegradable plant-derived and microbial thermoplastic polyester, as an eco-friendly binder alternative to toxic polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) for graphite negative electrodes in lithium-ion batteries. PHBV-bound graphite electrodes demonstrate highly comparable specific capacities, cycle stability, and lithium-ion diffusion coefficients to PVDF-bound baselines, indicating its high potential to improve sustainability in battery manufacturing.
green_strategy: Substitution of toxic, non-biodegradable synthetic fluorinated binders (PVDF) with a biodegradable, bio-derived, and compostable biopolymer (PHBV).
fabrication_method: Slurry casting of graphite (80 wt%), carbon black conductive additive (10 wt%), and PHBV binder (10 wt%) in chloroform (CHCl3) solvent onto a copper current collector, followed by drying under vacuum at 90 °C for 24 h.

performance_metrics.metric: Specific capacity at 36 mA/g after 100 cycles
performance_metrics.condition: At current density of 36 mA/g, potential range 0.005 V to 3.0 V vs Li/Li+

performance_metrics.metric: Capacity recovery on returning to initial rate
performance_metrics.condition: Returned to C/20 rate after high-rate testing (up to 10C)
Determined via Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT)
performance_metrics.comparison_baseline: similar order of magnitude as PVDF
performance_metrics.source_section_or_page: Page 6, top of left column / Page 5, Fig. 7

limitations: The current fabrication protocol requires chloroform (CHCl3) as a solvent to dissolve PHBV, which is a volatile, toxic chlorinated organic solvent.
PHBV exhibits lower adhesion strength to the copper current collector compared to PVDF, which may result in a loss of electrical contact over very long-term cycling.
Poor rate capability at high discharge rates (4C, 10C), though a similar decline is observed for the PVDF benchmark due to electrode thickness transport limitations.

figure_or_table_evidence.evidence_summary: Displays charge/discharge cycling performance of TIMREX_PHBV and TIMREX_PVDF at varying current densities, showing highly similar discharge capacity profiles.
Shows the extended cycle life of the TIMREX_PHBV electrode over 100 cycles at a current density of 36 mA/g, maintaining a specific capacity of 357 mAh/g.
Plots the chemical diffusion coefficient of lithium ions as a function of potential, illustrating that PHBV and PVDF binders yield comparable kinetic properties.

candidate_research_claims.claim_text: Biodegradable PHBV can successfully substitute PVDF as a binder for graphite anodes in lithium-ion batteries, delivering highly stable cycling with a specific capacity of 357 mAh/g and 99.1% capacity retention after 100 cycles.
candidate_research_claims.experimental_condition: Graphite anode with 10 wt% PHBV binder, cycled at 36 mA/g over 100 cycles
candidate_research_claims.limitation: The processing required chloroform (CHCl3) solvent, and the polymer has lower adhesion to copper than PVDF.
candidate_research_claims.citation_text: The specific capacity of TIMREX_PHBV electrode material was 357 mAh/g after 100 cycles. ... with the capacity retention of 99.1 % after the 100th cycle. On the basis of the presented results, it may be concluded that the PHBV binder is resistant to dissolution or decomposition in the electrolyte...

human_review_reason: The green binder (PHBV) slurry requires a chlorinated solvent (chloroform) rather than a green water-based solvent system, representing a critical processing trade-off. Additionally, key rate capacity comparison values in Fig. 3 require manual digitization.
